title: Writing - Part 2: Exercise 1
description: Choose one of the following writing tasks and write your answer in 220-260 words.

tasks.title: Report
tasks.prompt: A parliamentary select committee on the future of work has commissioned a report on working conditions in the gig economy. Your report should describe the main features of gig work that distinguish it from conventional employment, assess the consequences for workers' financial security and wellbeing, and recommend legislative measures that could better protect gig economy workers without eliminating the flexibility that many of them value.
tasks.modelAnswer:
**Report on Working Conditions in the Gig Economy: Characteristics, Consequences and Recommendations**

**Introduction**
This report describes the defining features of gig economy employment, assesses their consequences for workers' financial security and wellbeing, and recommends legislative measures to improve protections while preserving the flexibility that represents a genuine benefit for a significant proportion of the workforce. Evidence draws on academic labour market research, platform worker surveys and recent case law from UK and EU jurisdictions.

**Distinguishing Features of Gig Work**
Gig workers typically hold self-employed or independent contractor status, work on demand through digital platforms, and are compensated per task or hour without fixed contractual hours. They bear their own operational costs, receive no employer pension contributions, and have no statutory entitlement to sick pay, paid annual leave or redundancy protection.

**Consequences for Workers**
Income volatility is the most pervasive consequence, with earnings fluctuating substantially in response to demand, algorithmic allocation and competition. Approximately forty-three percent of full-time gig workers surveyed reported experiencing a month of income below the poverty threshold in the preceding year. Mental health impacts associated with financial uncertainty are well-evidenced and disproportionately affect those who rely on platform work as their primary income source.

**Recommendations**
Three reforms are recommended: extension of statutory sick pay and annual leave entitlement to all workers irrespective of employment classification; mandatory portable benefits accounts funded by platforms based on hours worked; and a minimum earnings floor applicable to algorithmic task allocation.

**Conclusion**
Protecting gig workers requires updating legislative frameworks to reflect modern labour realities, ensuring that flexibility is not used as a justification for transferring structural economic risk onto the least powerful participants in the labour market.

tasks.title: Email
tasks.prompt: You work as a freelance graphic designer and rely on several online platforms to find clients. You have recently experienced a period of unexplained account suspension on one platform, resulting in significant loss of income. Write an email to the platform's support team explaining what happened, describing the financial and professional impact, and requesting a prompt review of your account status, clarification of the reason for suspension and appropriate compensation.
tasks.modelAnswer:
Dear Support Team,

I am writing to request urgent assistance regarding the suspension of my account on the DesignHub platform, which has been inaccessible since 9 April without prior notice or explanation.

I have been an active member of the platform for three years, with a consistent five-star rating, no history of disputes and a completed project record of over two hundred and thirty assignments. On the morning of 9 April, I attempted to log into my account to deliver a completed project to a client and found that access had been revoked. I received no advance notification, no email explanation and no indication of which, if any, of the platform's terms of service I am alleged to have violated.

The impact has been significant. I had four active projects in progress at the time of suspension, totalling approximately £1,400 in contracted fees, none of which I have been able to complete or communicate about with clients through the platform. I have also missed two new client enquiries that I can only assume were routed to my account during this period. In addition to the direct financial loss, the reputational damage of appearing to have abandoned active projects is deeply concerning to me professionally.

I am requesting three things: immediate review of my account and reinstatement if no policy violation can be demonstrated; a written explanation of the reason for suspension; and compensation for the fees I have been unable to collect due to circumstances entirely outside my control.

I would appreciate a response within forty-eight hours.

Yours faithfully,
Tomáš Horáček
Freelance Graphic Designer

tasks.title: Review
tasks.prompt: You have recently read a book called 'Platform Power: How Tech Companies Are Reshaping Work, Wealth and Society' which argues that gig economy platforms have fundamentally altered the balance of power between capital and labour, concentrating wealth among platform owners while offloading risk onto millions of low-paid workers, and that only coordinated political and regulatory action can reverse these trends. Write a review for an economics and society journal evaluating the strength of the book's analysis, its use of evidence, and whether its prescriptions are convincing.
tasks.modelAnswer:
**Incisive Diagnosis, Ambitious Prescription: A Review of 'Platform Power'**

In 'Platform Power', labour economist Professor Adaeze Obi delivers one of the most comprehensive and rigorously argued analyses of the gig economy's political economy to have appeared in recent years. Her central contention — that digital labour platforms represent not a neutral technological innovation but a deliberate restructuring of the employment relationship that transfers risk from capital to labour while concentrating profits at the apex of platform ownership — is neither original nor controversial in academic circles, but it has rarely been made with such analytical precision for a general readership.

The book's empirical foundation is robust. Obi draws on earnings data from multiple platforms across a dozen countries, longitudinal studies of gig workers' income trajectories, and detailed analysis of the legal strategies through which platform companies have resisted worker classification as employees. The comparative chapters examining divergent regulatory responses in France, California and the United Kingdom are particularly illuminating, demonstrating that the outcome of these battles is shaped by political choices rather than technological inevitability.

The prescriptive final section — advocating for international coordination between labour regulators, portable benefits systems and algorithmic transparency legislation — is ambitious, and the author is candid about the political obstacles involved. Some readers may find the proposals underdeveloped relative to the power of the preceding diagnosis.

Nevertheless, 'Platform Power' is an outstanding contribution to a debate of genuine public importance, and essential reading for economists, labour lawyers and policymakers engaged with the future of work.
